coredata.dc:title: A smartphone-assisted optosensing platform based on chromium-based metal-organic framework signal amplification for ultrasensitive and real-time determination of oxytetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Xuelian
Cao, Yichuan
Cai, Lin
Wang, Haiyang
Fang, Guozhen
Wang, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: Ultrasensitive and onsite detection of oxytetracycline (OTC) is of vital significance for ensuring public health. Herein, a novel and versatile fluorescence biomimetic nanosensor, Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP, was elaborately tailored for the assay of OTC. MIL-101 with extraordinarily high surface area and porosity, as a favorable supporter, suppressed self-quenching of Mg,N-CDs and boosted mass transfer rate, realizing signal amplification. As an ultrasensitive signal transducer, high luminescent Mg,N-CDs yielded conspicuous fluorescence responses for OTC, enhancing the sensitivity of Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP. High-affinity imprinting sites further endowed Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP with superior anti-interference ability and reusability. Given prominent merits, Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP demonstrated a good linear range (0.05–40 μg mL−1) with a lower limit of detection (16.8 ng mL−1), supplying high accessibility to realize ultrasensitive and highly selective measurement of OTC in samples. Additionally, to attain precise onsite profiling of OTC, an intelligent sensing platform was developed by integrating Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP with a portable smartphone-assisted optical device. As both signal reader and analyzer, smartphone can instantly capture concentration-dependent fluorescent images and accurately digitize them, accomplishing quantitative analysis of OTC. More delightfully, the portable platform was utilized for visual determination of OTC in milk samples with satisfactory results, offering a promising tool for the high-performance onsite evaluation of food safety and environmental health.

(G) FT-IR spectra of Mg,N-CDs, MIL-101, Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP and Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@NIP. (H) Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns of MIL-101, Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP and Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@NIP. (A) The changes in the fluorescence intensity of Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP and Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@NIP caused by chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), tetracycline (TC), doxycycline (DOX), chloroamphenicol (CAP) and kanamycin (KAN). (B) The effect of CTC, TC, DOX, CAP and KAN (100 μg mL−1) on the binding of OTC (20 μg mL−1) to Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP. (C) Influence of coexisting interferences (100 μg mL−1) on the fluorescence intensity ratio of Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP in the absence or presence of OTC (20 μg mL−1). (D) Relevant fluorescence photos of Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP-based sensing system in the presence of OTC or coexisting interferences under 365 nm UV lamp illumination.

(A) Schematic diagram of using the developed smartphone-integrated sensing platform to detect OTC. (B) Fluorescence images captured by smartphone of Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP upon the addition of diverse concentrations of OTC under UV lamp (λ = 365 nm). (C) Linear relationship between I
mean and the OTC concentrations (OTC concentrations: 0.09, 0.5, 2.5, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40 μg mL−1). (D) The ratio of F 0/F and I mean against the OTC concentrations.

head.ce:title: A smartphone-assisted optosensing platform based on chromium-based metal-organic framework signal amplification for ultrasensitive and real-time determination of oxytetracycline

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasensitive and onsite detection of oxytetracycline (OTC) is of vital significance for ensuring public health. Herein, a novel and versatile fluorescence biomimetic nanosensor, Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP, was elaborately tailored for the assay of OTC. MIL-101 with extraordinarily high surface area and porosity, as a favorable supporter, suppressed self-quenching of Mg,N-CDs and boosted mass transfer rate, realizing signal amplification. As an ultrasensitive signal transducer, high luminescent Mg,N-CDs yielded conspicuous fluorescence responses for OTC, enhancing the sensitivity of Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP. High-affinity imprinting sites further endowed Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP with superior anti-interference ability and reusability. Given prominent merits, Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP demonstrated a good linear range (0.05–40 μg mL−1) with a lower limit of detection (16.8 ng mL−1), supplying high accessibility to realize ultrasensitive and highly selective measurement of OTC in samples. Additionally, to attain precise onsite profiling of OTC, an intelligent sensing platform was developed by integrating Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP with a portable smartphone-assisted optical device. As both signal reader and analyzer, smartphone can instantly capture concentration-dependent fluorescent images and accurately digitize them, accomplishing quantitative analysis of OTC. More delightfully, the portable platform was utilized for visual determination of OTC in milk samples with satisfactory results, offering a promising tool for the high-performance onsite evaluation of food safety and environmental health.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescence metal-organic frameworks (LMOFs), as an enchanting class of porous organic-inorganic hybrid materials, have found promising applications in the fields of sensing on account of high surface area and prominent luminescent properties (Wu et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018). However, the universally developed LMOFs are synthesized in organic solvent systems, resulting in poor water stability (Li et al., 2019; Qiao et al., 2021; Shu et al., 2021). Additionally, some LMOFs prepared by room temperature stirring strategy are susceptible to inherent drawbacks of poor acid and alkali resistance and thermal instability (Lin et al., 2014; Tan et al., 2020). To address the before-mentioned challenge, MIL-101, as the most common MIL-type MOFs consisting of dicarboxylate ligands with Cr2+, has been proposed to synthesize LMOFs owing to its stability toward high temperatures and water (Alivand et al., 2021; Cha et al., 2019). For instance, MIL-101@amine-CQDs, a novel hybrid fluorescent probe with superior stability and satisfactory sensing performance, were developed and employed to detect 6-mercaptopurine (Mousavi et al., 2020). Hence, MIL-101 with water resistance and high thermal stability was more favorable as a promising host to synthesize the fascinating LMOFs (Karmakar et al., 2019). Nevertheless, the previously developed LMOFs-based sensors still have certain limitations such as poor sensitivity in applications, because the encapsulated luminescent nanoparticles suffer from low fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) (Huang et al., 2018). Therefore, exploring luminescent nanoparticles with excellent photoluminescence properties and high FLQY will be an important approach for constructing high-performance LMOFs.

Carbon dots (CDs), an up-and-coming fluorescent nanomaterial, have aroused tremendous concerns due to the noteworthy superiorities of tunable emission wavelength, high luminescence efficiency and surface tailorability (W. Li et al., 2021; X. Li et al., 2021; Long et al., 2021). Compared with traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, CDs possess the prominent virtues of low-toxicity, satisfactory biocompatibility and easy preparation (Tan et al., 2020). In spite of the fact that the impressive performance of CDs has been displayed, it is still a challenge for developing CDs-based sensors with desirable sensitivity owing to the relatively finite fluorescence quantum yield of CDs (Lu et al., 2018). Fortunately, as a simple and effective modification nanotechnology, element doping has been diffusely used to ameliorate the photoluminescence properties of CDs (Alaghmandfard et al., 2021). Recently, in light of the magnesium and nitrogen co-doped strategy, Peng et al. (2019) designed and synthesized the novel carbon dots (Mg,N-CDs) with high FLQY (33%) and fascinating physicochemical stability. As a common dopant atom, nitrogen atom has been utilized extensively for the construction of carbon dots, which can not only improve the fluorescence efficiency, but also yield more active sites (amino group) to ameliorate the recognition ability for target molecules. On the other hand, the chelation between magnesium ions and carboxyl groups of citric acid can effectively inhibit the excessive consumption of carboxyl groups during carbonization, which further enhanced the fluorescence efficiency of carbon dots. Encouraged by the prominent performance, Mg,N-CDs are taken as the promising guest materials for the construction of LMOFs-based sensors. However, the poor selectivity and reusability of the currently reported sensors based on LMOFs bring about a considerable challenge for monitoring trace target molecules in complex foods. Thence, the development of LMOFs-based nanocomposites with distinguished selectivity will be a significant strategy to facilitate the accurate measurement of target molecules.

Molecular imprinting technique (MIT), as a cost-effective yet efficient approach for enhancing the specific binding capacity of materials, has been proverbially applied to synthesize molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) with tailor-made sites (Malik et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2021). As recognition units, MIPs have been exploited for the detection of various organic compounds due to simplicity of synthesis, high specificity and excellent stability (Arabi et al., 2020). However, MIPs synthesized using bulk imprinting generally suffer from difficult elution, relatively limited imprinting efficiency and lengthy response time. To overcome the shortcomings referenced above, LMOFs functioning as the supporter and signal transducer have been hotly utilized for the preparation of core-shell molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensors (Liu et al., 2021). For example, by adopting terbium metal-organic frameworks as the supporter and sensing element, MIP@TbMOF-76, a sensitive and reliable molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor, was designed and applied to the selective measurement of cefixime (Eskandari et al., 2018). Amiripour and co-workers (Amiripour et al., 2021) proposed an efficient strategy to fabricate the core-shell fluorescent sensor (MIP/Zr-LMOF) by polymerizing imprinting layer on the surface of Zr-LMOF with the porous structure, achieving the ultrasensitive detection and specific recognition of chloramphenicol. Compared to the bulk imprinting polymers, MIP@LMOFs composites hold the appealing merits of larger binding capacity, faster adsorption kinetics and higher sensitivity, which benefited from the high surface area, specific porous structure and superior signal transduction capabilities of LMOFs. Therefore, the fluorescence strategy based on the integration of MIPs with LMOFs furnishes an efficient, sensitive, and specific monitoring platform for food quality and safety control, such as trace detection of residual antibiotics.

In recent years, the overuse of antibiotics has attracted growing attention because of the serious adverse effects on the living environment and human health (Ye et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2019). Among them, oxytetracycline (OTC), a sort of tetracycline antibiotic that possessed broad-spectrum antibacterial activity, is extensively utilized for the therapy of various bacterial infections in animal husbandry owing to its cheapness and efficiency (Wang et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2021). However, high usage of OTC resulted in drug residues in foodstuffs, posing a significant threat to human health, such as chronic toxicity, liver damage, anaphylactic reaction as well as bacterial resistance (J. Yang et al., 2020; K. Yang et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2021). In this context, several countries including European Union (EU) and China have stipulated the MRLs of 100 ng mL−1 for OTC in milk (J. Yang et al., 2020; K. Yang et al., 2020). Up to now, a series of mature analytical techniques for the quantification detection of OTC have been established, involving liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) (Vidal et al., 2009), enzyme-linked immunoassay (Wang et al., 2019), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Lv et al., 2012), fluorescence spectrophotometry (Zhao et al., 2021) and so forth. Among these, fluorometry is regarded as a desirable approach on account of its marvelous advantages like high sensitivity, operability and rapidity. Unfortunately, the implementation of fluorescence assay relies on an expensive and bulky fluorophotometer, which is not available for the precise real-time monitoring of OTC (Zhu et al., 2021), especially in emergency situations. Taking account of this actuality, there is an imminent requirement to manufacture intelligent and portable optical sensing devices for accurate on-site detection of trace OTC in environmental and food samples.

Smartphones as potential analysis implements equipped with various types of processor functions and high-performance cameras have drawn extensive attention because of their portability, simple operation and faster processing speed (X. Li et al., 2021; W. Li et al., 2021; Noor and Krull, 2014). As sample-to-answer analyzers, smartphones can capture fluorescent color images with the built-in camera and then convert the images into the corresponding digital values through professional software programs developed by third-party service providers, which offer promising sensing platforms for point-of-care testing (X. Li et al., 2021; W. Li et al., 2021; Ye et al., 2020). Till now, a few portable smartphone-assisted optical analytical devices have been widely developed and implemented for the rapid real-time detection of different targets (Sun et al., 2021). For instance, Du and co-workers (Zeinhom et al., 2018) reported a simple and portable fluorescence device based on a smartphone, which realized in-situ and accurate quantitation analysis of E. coli O157:H7 in yogurt and egg. More recently, Zhu et al. (2021) employed red-emission carbon dots (RCDs) as a response signal to devise and fabricate a portable smartphone-integrated optosensing platform, which was successfully utilized for on-site visualization and rapid detection of pyrethroids. Despite the aforementioned progress, the key to the successful realization of POCT application by the smartphone-assisted fluorescence strategy is that the developed fluorescence nanosensor must possess a sensitive fluorescence response to the target molecule, which is accompanied by a distinct fluorescence color change.

Inspired by the above circumstances, herein, a simple yet valid fluorescence strategy based on Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP was proposed for the determination of OTC. In this strategy, MIL-101 with extraordinarily high surface area and porosity was selected as a host matrix to encapsulate Mg,N-CDs (the guest), which contributed to suppressing the self-quenching of Mg,N-CDs created by aggregation, thereby achieving signal amplification. On the other hand, MIL-101 can remarkably increase OTC accessibility to tailor-made imprinting sites, ameliorating the mass transfer rate and binding efficiency of the nanosensor. Meanwhile, the bright blue Mg,N-CDs with high luminescence efficiency and fascinating physicochemical stability were introduced as a fluorescent response signal, which improved the sensitivity of the nanosensor. The decoration of imprinting layer with OTC-imprinted sites enabled the resulting sensor to demonstrate superior binding efficiency, prominent selectivity and satisfying reusability. Thanks to the integration of the significant advantages from MIL-101, Mg,N-CDs and imprinting layer, the developed nanosensor not only presented a high sensitivity fluorescence response towards OTC, but also displayed the prominent anti-interference capacity for the monitoring of OTC in complex samples. More importantly, aiming at achieving accurate onsite determination in applications, a smart sensing platform by introducing the portable smartphone-integrated optical device was further constructed. Delightfully, the developed portable optosensing platform achieved the onsite quantitative analysis of OTC in actual samples with satisfactory results.

Chromium nitrate nonahydrate (Cr(NO3)3 ▪ 9 H2O), terephthalic acid (H2BDC), APTES, TEOS, and OTC were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Magnesium hydroxide, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), citric acid and ethylenediamine were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were determined by a Hitachi F-7100 (Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were detected on a UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The dimensions of the materials were detected on a Mastersizer 3000 (Mastersizer, Britain). Functional groups on the surface of materials were recorded on a Tensor-37 fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). S4800 scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi, Japan) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan) were performed to observe the morphology and structure.

MIL-101 was synthesized according to a previously reported method with slight modifications (Alivand et al., 2021; Fe´rey et al., 2005). First, Cr(NO3)3 ▪ 9 H2O (10 mmol) and H2BDC (10 mmol) were dispersed uniformly in 48 mL of ultrapure water and stirred vigorously for 30 min. Next, 2 mL of HF (5 M) was added dropwise to the above reaction system. The mixture was then transferred into a 100 mL autoclave, which was reacted at 220 ℃ for 8 h. After cooling, the resulting solution was washed with DMF and ethanol for purification. Finally, green crystals (MIL-101) were attained through drying overnight.

Mg,N-CDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method according to our previous work (Peng et al., 2019). Briefly, citric acid (10 g) was dispersed in ultrapure water (40 mL), and then thoroughly mixed with Mg(OH)2 (4.2 g) and ethylenediamine (5 mL) under vigorous stirring. Subsequently, the mixed solution was transferred into a 100 mL autoclave, which was reacted at 200 ℃ for 3 h. After cooling in ambient air, the obtained yellowish solution was further purified via dialysis and lyophilization. The final powder was stored at 4 ℃ for further application.

Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP was prepared based on a modified sol-gel method (Wei et al., 2019). Generally, the as-synthesized MIL-101 (50 mg) and Mg,N-CDs (10 mg) were first mixed in 9 mL of ethanol by continuous mechanical stirring for 2 h. Meanwhile, OTC (0.1 mmol) and APTES (0.6 mmol) were dispersed in 9 mL of ethanol and pre-polymerized for 2 h. After mixing the above two parts of the solution, TEOS (2 mmol) and HOAc (2 mL, 0.1 mM) were sequentially added to the mixed solution and dispersed uniformly under stirring. Subsequently, the oxygen in the system was removed by nitrogen blowing for 15 min, and the mixture was reacted at ambient temperature for 8 h. After polymerization, the resulting polymer was washed using a mixture of ethanol and water (v/v, 1:1). Finally, Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP powders were attained through drying. Meanwhile, the non-imprinted polymer (Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@NIP) was prepared following the same process described above without OTC. Besides, Mg,N-CDs@MIP was prepared under equivalent conditions, except that no MIL-101 was introduced as the supporter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for fluorescence measurement of OTC
ce:section.ce:para: In a general FL measurement procedure, MIP (Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP) suspension (0.2 mg mL−1) was first prepared by mixing 1 mg of MIP and 5 mL of PBS buffer solution (pH = 7). Then various concentrations of OTC standards were added to MIP suspension (v/v, 1:1) and allowed to incubate for 15 min at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra in the range of 390–600 nm were measured at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Three repeated measurements were conducted in every experiment. The comparison systems were performed by introducing NIP (Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@NIP) or Mg,N-CDs@MIP to replace MIP according to the above approach.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The construction of the portable optical device for onsite monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that the detection system will be disturbed by the light source, distance and so forth in the implementation, a compact optical device was designed and exploited. The primary components of the optical device were portrayed in Fig. S1. 365 nm LED light activated with a portable power bank, as an excitation light source, was fixed on the lamp holder to excite Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP composite for generating fluorescence signals. To eliminate the influence created by the distance between sample and excitation beam, the sample slot was installed in the optical device. Simultaneously, a 400 nm filter was placed between the smartphone and the before-mentioned sample slot, which can effectively avoid the interference yielded by the excitation beam during the onsite monitoring process. As the signal reader and analyzer, a smartphone was applied to extract the RGB values from the fluorescent color images through professional software programs (Color Picker APP) and convert them into grayscale values (I), where I was determined according to the formula I = 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114B (L. Li et al., 2018; H. Li et al., 2018; Wong et al., 2014). To further improve the detection accuracy, the mean value (I mean), is given by the equation I mean = (I blank - I test) / I blank, was used for the quantitative analysis (Xiao et al., 2020). Additionally, a light-proof cassette comprised of black metal plate materials, as a portable carrier, was utilized to integrate the aforementioned components for accomplishing the real-time testing of OTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of OTC in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess potential practicability of fluorescence sensing approach based on Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP, the commercial milk was selected as the real samples, and an appropriate amount of OTC was spiked into the milk samples. The milk samples were treated according to a previous report with slight modifications (Qian et al., 2019). Briefly, 4 mL of acetonitrile was first mixed with 1 g milk samples containing different OTC concentrations (1 μg mL−1, 10 μg mL−1, 30 μg mL−1) to precipitate proteins. After vortexing for 5 min, the mixed samples were centrifuged and then filtered using microfiltration membranes to eliminate residual protein. Finally, the treated samples were dried and redispersed with PBS buffer solution (1 mL) for fluorescence analysis.
The HPLC analysis procedure was devised and executed following the standard (GB/T 22990–2008).

As illustrated in Scheme 1, an innovative core-shell fluorescent nanosensor was designed and fabricated by a simple yet valid sol-gel technology. In this protocol, MIL-101 with extraordinarily high surface area and porosity was prepared through a self-assembly strategy using chromium nitrate and terephthalic acid as the precursors. Meanwhile, Mg,N-CDs were synthesized via the one-pot hydrothermal approach, which demonstrated extremely bright blue fluorescence and superior water stability. Subsequently, the as-synthesized Mg,N-CDs as fluorophores were encapsulated into MIL-101 (the host) to form a composite material, Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101, with a facile room-temperature stirring strategy. Next, the polymerization process was executed in the presence of Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101 (supporter and signal transducer), OTC (imprinted template), APTES (functional monomer) and TEOS (cross-linking agent). After removal of the imprinted OTC with an appropriate eluent, Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP with specific recognition cavities matched to OTC in shape, size and functional group was finally acquired. Herein, the introduction of MIL-101 offered a favorable supporter for the encapsulation of Mg,N-CDs and the formation of OTC-imprinted sites, which was conducive to boosting the adsorption capacity of the nanosensor, realizing signal amplification. Simultaneously, the ultra-small size of Mg,N-CDs facilitated its efficient encapsulation, overcoming the multi-step modification of the nanosensor. Moreover, as the signal tag, Mg,N-CDs with high FLQY can dramatically enhance the sensitivity and accuracy of the nanosensor. The transparent silicon-based imprinting layer, as the recognition unit, not only maintained the sensitive fluorescence signal of LMOFs, but also further improved the anti-interference ability of the nanosensor. Therefore, the as-synthesized fluorescent nanosensor integrating the advantages of Mg,N-CDs, MIL-101 and imprinting layer provided a new strategy for the rapid and efficient detection of OTC. Proverbially, the imprinting process dominated the sensing performance of the proposed nanosensor. To attain Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP with superior specific binding ability and sensitive fluorescence response for OTC, the corresponding synthesis conditions were optimized in Tables S1-S4.

The morphologies and structures of Mg,N-CDs, MIL-101, MIP (Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP) and NIP (Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@NIP) were explored through TEM and SEM, respectively. As delineated in Fig. S2, the resulting Mg,N-CDs demonstrated a good dispersion in aqueous solution and comparatively uniform morphology with a mean size of 3 nm, which facilitated its valid encapsulation. As displayed in Fig. 1A and D, the pure MIL-101 presented a regular octahedral crystalline structure with an average size of ∼400 nm (Alivand et al., 2021). And the as-synthesized MIL-101 had a smooth surface, where no sign of needle-like H2BDC crystals was found. These results indicated that MIL-101 has been successfully synthesized and purified. As portrayed in Fig. 1B and C, the particle size of the polymers (MIP and NIP) was increased to approximately 650 nm, but the polymers still maintained the structure of MIL-101. Compared with MIL-101, the surface of the polymers became rougher owing to the introduction of the imprinting layer. Additionally, it can be seen from TEM images (Fig. 1E and F) that polymers had an obvious core-shell structure, which verified the successful formation of the polymers. Simultaneously, the corresponding elemental mapping images (Fig. S3) exhibited that the characteristic elements (C、O、N、Cr、Mg、Si) were contained in the composite material (MIP), which preliminarily manifested that the fluorescent nanosensor was triumphantly prepared.

The materials (Mg,N-CDs, MIL-101, MIP and NIP) were investigated utilizing FT-IR spectroscopy to identify surface functional groups. As depicted in Fig. 1G, for MIL-101, the absorption peaks at 1407 cm−1 and 574 cm−1 were attributed to the vibrations of O-C-O and Cr-O reflecting the formation of MIL-101. The characteristic peak at 1669 cm−1 corresponding to the unreacted ligand H2BDC (CO) was not found in MIL-101, implying that MIL-101 was successfully prepared and purified (Alivand et al., 2019; Su et al., 2020). Compared with MIL-101, a new absorption band (3312 cm−1) associated with the vibration of O-H and N-H originating from Mg,N-CDs emerged in NIP, indicating that Mg,N-CDs were introduced into the composite material. The characteristic peak at 2971 cm−1 related to antisymmetric stretching vibration of the C-H bond in the silane reagent also appeared in NIP (Huang et al., 2020). Meanwhile, the distinct absorption bands deriving from Si-O-Si (1050 cm−1) and Si-O (789 and 457 cm−1) were observed in NIP, meaning that a wrapped imprinting layer was formed on the surface of LMOFs after the hydrolysis condensation of APTES and TEOS. For FT-IR spectroscopy of OTC (Fig. S4), a typical peak around 3362 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of -OH in OTC (curve a), and such sharp absorption peak may due to the presence of intramolecular hydrogen bonds between group -OH and CO in OTC molecular (Xu et al., 2019). With the introduction of functional monomer APTES, intermolecular hydrogen bonds between OTC and APTES appeared and occupied a dominant position, which led to the broadening of the absorption peak (-OH) in the composite material before eluting OTC (curve b). However, after removing of OTC (curve c), the characteristic absorption peaks of OTC disappearance in MIP. Furthermore, MIP was basically consistent with NIP in the intensity and position of the absorption peaks. The data underlined that Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP-based nanosensor was successfully synthesized.

The crystal structures of the as-prepared materials were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As displayed in Fig. 1H, the significant characteristic absorption peaks (2θ = 5.98°, 8.4°, 9.0°, 16.4°) in the diffraction pattern of MIL-101 were in agreement with the reported references, which illustrated that the successful preparation of MIL-101 with the high crystallinity (Su et al., 2020). Meanwhile, the negligible peaks (2θ = 17.4°, 25.2° and 27.9°), as a sign of recrystallized H2BDC (Alivand et al., 2019), verified the efficient removal of the unreacted H2BDC after multi-step purification steps. For MIP and NIP, the diffraction peaks of MIL-101 became inconspicuous in the polymers curves because of the decoration of imprinting layer. Noticeably, the diffraction peak (2θ = 22°) associated with Mg,N-CDs was essentially absent in the polymers patterns, which was mainly attributed to the low content of Mg,N-CDs and the efficient encapsulation of Mg,N-CDs into MIL-101 (Alivand et al., 2021; Mousavi et al., 2020). The above results further confirmed the successful formation of Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP.

The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms at 77 K of MIL-101, MIP and NIP were presented (Fig. 1I) to validate the porosity. For the pure MIL-101, type-IV isotherm according to the IUPAC categorization was observed, indicating that MIL-101 had a hierarchical micro-mesoporous structure. The specific surface areas of the materials were further investigated by BET nitrogen adsorption method. The corresponding surface areas of MIL-101, MIP and NIP were 2528 m2 g−1, 64 m2 g−1 and 33 m2 g−1. Compared with MIL-101, the surface area of MIP and NIP evidently decreased, which was attributed to the formation of imprinting layer on the surface of MIL-101 (K. Yang et al., 2020; J. Yang et al., 2020).

Together the before-mentioned results clarified that an FL nanosensor (Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP) was successfully constructed by coupling Mg,N-CDs-functionalized MIL-101 with molecularly imprinted polymers.

The luminescence properties of as-synthesized materials were extensively studied. As portrayed in Fig. 2A, Mg,N-CDs exhibited a broad emission peak centered at 445 nm when excited at 350 nm. Additionally, compared with N-CDs (∼14%), Mg,N-CDs possessed a higher FLQY (∼33%) on account of the magnesium and nitrogen co-doped strategy. It is well known that the introduction of nitrogen as a heteroatom can enhance the fluorescence efficiency of carbon dots by adjusting the electron distribution and yielding more active sites. On the other hand, the chelation effect of Mg2+ was beneficial to avoid the excessive consumption of carboxyl groups during carbonization, which further improved the FLQY of Mg,N-CDs (Peng et al., 2019). However, FL spectrum of MIL-101 was basically a straight line under identical conditions, manifesting that MIL-101 had a negligible effect on the luminescence properties of the composites. Fig. 2A demonstrated that Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP presented a blue emission with a maximum at 445 nm, revealing that the nanosensor maintained the typical PL property of Mg,N-CDs. However, it is worth noting that the fluorescence intensity of the nanosensor was slightly lower than that of Mg,N-CDs due to the decoration of MIP layer. The above results meant that the nanosensor featured with a characteristic fluorescence emission peak (445 nm) was successfully developed.

The feasibility of the nanosensor was first investigated by introducing OTC into the detection system. The fluorescence intensity of the polymers (MIP and NIP) binding to OTC, as displayed in Fig. 2B, was validly quenched. Apparently, compared with NIP, MIP exhibited a more remarkable FL response, which was ascribed to the stronger specificity and affinity of MIP for OTC. After removing the imprinted OTC, the FL intensity of MIP recovered to nearly that of NIP, denoting that the as-constructed nanosensor (Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP) possessed good feasibility for the measurement of OTC.

The possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of the developed nanosensor toward OTC was systematically discussed and verified. To enunciate the OTC-induced FL quenching mechanism, UV–vis absorption spectrum of OTC, FL excitation and emission spectra of Mg,N-CDs were recorded in Fig. 2C. Apparently, there was negligible overlap between UV–vis absorption spectrum of OTC and FL emission spectrum of Mg,N-CDs, whereas FL excitation spectrum of Mg,N-CDs partially overlapped with UV–vis absorption spectrum of OTC. The results preliminarily illustrated that the fluorescence quenching of Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP was caused by IFE instead of FRET. To further reveal the quenching principle, a related verification was carried out by measuring fluorescence lifetime. As delineated in Fig. S5, the fluorescence lifetime of the sensing system displayed a slight fluctuation from 6.54 ns to 6.42 ns upon the addition of OTC, which further confirmed that OTC-triggered fluorescence quenching of the nanosensor was primarily ascribed to IFE (Liang et al., 2016; Lin et al., 2016).

Aiming at obtaining the superior sensing performance of the nanosensor (Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP), the relevant experimental parameters affecting detection sensitivity were optimized, including solution pH and response time. First, the influence of pH on the FL intensity of the nanosensor and the binding efficiency of the nanosensor towards OTC was investigated in the range of 5–9. It can be seen from Fig. 2D that the fluorescence intensity of the nanosensor first increased and then decreased with the increase of pH from 5 to 9. Evidently, the highest fluorescence intensity appeared at pH = 7. Meanwhile, upon introduction of OTC, the sensing system achieved the strongest fluorescence response at pH = 7, indicating the highest binding efficiency of the nanosensor for OTC was reached. Therefore, the neutral condition (pH = 7) as the optimal condition was used for the follow-up fluorescence sensing. Subsequently, the response time was optimized through measuring the FL changes of the polymers (MIP and NIP). As illustrated in Fig. 2E, the fluorescence response of polymers increased rapidly and reached a maximum at 15 min. Afterward, F0/F value of the polymers maintained stable, meaning that the binding of the polymers to OTC achieved equilibrium at 15 min. Thus, 15 min as the optimal reaction time was applied to the subsequent experiments. Noticeably, compared with NIP, MIP exhibited a higher fluorescence response and stronger binding efficiency towards OTC, which was ascribed to the presence of high-affinity imprinting sites. As shown in Fig. S6, FL response of Mg,N-CDs@MIP achieved balance at 50 min, which was 3.3-fold that of the established nanosensor (15 min). Furthermore, the nanosensor had a stronger quenching efficiency than Mg,N-CDs@MIP under the identical reaction time. As a consequence, the introduction of MIL-101 as a supporter can endow the nanosensor with the distinguished mass transfer rate and binding ability, shortening sample response time and achieving signal amplification.

Under the most favorable sensing conditions, the fluorescence emissions of the sensing system were investigated by exposing the polymers (MIP and NIP) to various concentrations of OTC standards. As portrayed in Fig. 3A, FL intensity of MIP was progressively declined along with increasing concentration of OTC. Apparently, NIP had a similar trend to MIP. However, MIP exhibited more remarkable fluorescence quenching than NIP over the same range of concentration, which was attributed to the existence of the tailor-made recognition sites in MIP. Additionally, the relationship between FL quenching degree (F
0 /F) with the concentration of OTC can be described by Stern-Volmer equation:
F 0/F = K SV C OTC + 1

In which K SV is the fluorescence quenching constant. F 0 and F represent fluorescence intensity of the polymers in the absence and presence of OTC, respectively, C OTC is the concentration of OTC. Imprinting factor (IF, IF = K sv,MIP/K sv,NIP), as a measurement index, was employed to evaluate the selective recognition ability of the nanosensor to target molecules (OTC).

As depicted in Fig. 3C, FL quenching degree of MIP was closely linear with the concentration of OTC in the range of 0.05–40 μg mL−1. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 16.8 ng mL−1 according to the standard 3σ/S (Gan et al., 2021; Li et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2020), which was low enough for MRLs (100 ng mL−1) of OTC established by China and the EU. Compared with NIP (Fig. 3B and Fig. S7), MIP demonstrated a stronger FL quenching degree and a more distinguishable fluorescent color variation under the same amount of OTC, which denoted that MIP possessed the higher selectivity to OTC because of the specific binding sites. The superior sensing performance of MIP towards OTC was validated by comparing slopes of the corresponding standard linear equations (Fig. 3C and D) of MIP (F 0/F = 0.05 C OTC + 1.004, R 2 = 0.998) and NIP (F 0/F = 0.0093 C OTC + 1.0109, R 2 = 0.994). More delightfully, the high IF was calculated to be 5.38, which further emphasized that Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP enjoyed the exceptional selective recognition capacity for OTC.

For comparison, the dose response of Mg,N-CDs@MIP towards OTC was monitored and recorded in Fig. S8. Evidently, F 0/F of Mg,N-CDs@MIP displayed a linear correlation with OTC concentration in the range of 0.125–40 μg mL−1, and the LOD was 41.3 ng mL−1. Compared with Mg,N-CDs@MIP (Fig. S8 and S9), Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP presented a lower LOD (16.8 ng mL−1) and a wider linear range (0.05–40 μg mL−1) and a more remarkable color evolution, which originated from the introduction of MIL-101 (Fu et al., 2022). As a supporter, MIL-101 with high porosity and large surface area contributed to the formation of a good many imprinting sites on the surface of the nanosensor, which supplied higher accessibility to realize the highly sensitive measurement at a low concentration of OTC. On the other hand, MIL-101 inhibited the self-quenching of Mg,N-CDs induced by aggregation, thereby achieving signal amplification. The above comparison results displayed that the proposed nanosensor was more conducive to the sensitive evaluation of OTC.

Selectivity of the as-prepared nanosensor was estimated by monitoring the fluorescence change of system in the presence OTC or structurally related antibiotics. To comprehend selective recognition ability of Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP towards the template OTC, Fig. S10 illustrated the chemical structure of before-mentioned antibiotics. As depicted in Fig. 4A, compared with other antibiotics, only OTC yielded the more prominent FL change to Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP, which was mainly due to the synergistic effect of the fluorescence selectivity that came from Mg,N-CDs and the specificity of OTC-imprinted cavities. Note that CTC, TC and DOX were structurally very closer to OTC, but Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP exhibited a slight fluorescence quenching degree (F
0 /F) for CTC, TC and DOX. The aforementioned results revealed that the related antibiotics (CTC, TC, DOX, CAP and KAN) do not structurally correspond to OTC-imprinted sites. In other words, there were no tailor-made cavities that were completely complementary to the size, shape and functional groups of the related antibiotics in the nanosensor. Accordingly, it is difficult for Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP to selectively recognize and combine with these analogs during the adsorption process (Jiang et al., 2021; Tawfik et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2018). On the other hand, the fluorescence selectivity derived from Mg,N-CDs enabled the nanosensor to exhibit a stronger fluorescence change for OTC (H. Li et al., 2018; L. Li et al., 2018; Qian et al., 2019). Therefore, the double screening function based on imprinted layer and Mg,N-CDs enabled the sensor to display satisfactory selective binding ability for OTC. Additionally, Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@NIP presented a lower FL quenching efficiency towards analytes than Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP because of the lack of specific recognition sites, manifesting that the introduction of imprinting sites with strong affinity and selectivity enabled Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP-based sensor to achieve specific recognition of OTC.

To further assess the binding capacity of the nanosensor to OTC in the coexistence system, competitive adsorption experiments were executed by introducing the potential antibiotics (CTC, TC, DOX, CAP and KAN) into OTC-containing solution. As shown in Fig. 4B, the coexisting antibiotics induced the insignificant fluorescence change for Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP even at a concentration that was 5-fold that of OTC, manifesting that the developed nanosensor maintained an favorable recognition ability for OTC in complex environment due to the presence of tailor-made sites. OTC-imprinted sites were beneficial to preferentially bind target molecules (OTC) and avoid the non-specific adsorption for coexisting antibiotics. On the other hand, the fluorescence selectivity derived from Mg,N-CDs enabled the nanosensor to exhibit the more charming fluorescence change for OTC. Therefore, the synergistic effect of imprinting layer and Mg,N-CDs endowed the resulting nanosensor with satisfactory specificity and selectivity.

Taking account of the complexity of samples, the anti-interference ability of the nanosensor was in-depth investigated by adding a series of possible interfering analytes (100 μg mL−1 that was approximately 5-fold of OTC), involving saccharides, amino acids and metal ions. As illustrated in Fig. 4C, the interfering analytes only triggered tiny fluorescence variation even in a high concentration (5-fold OTC), which was basically in accordance with the blank group. However, upon the introduction of OTC, the sensing system exhibited conspicuous fluorescence quenching, revealing that coexisting interfering analytes had a negligible influence on FL determination of OTC. Additionally, OTC caused a more pronounced fluorescence color change compared to other analytes (Fig. 4D) under a 365 nm UV lamp. As a consequence, the favorable anti-interference ability of the nanosensor in complex environment supplied a prerequisite for the specific identification of OTC in real samples.

The stability of the as-synthesized nanosensor against continuous ultraviolet irradiation and storage time were examined. As presented in Fig. S11A, the fluorescence intensity of the nanosensor exhibited a negligible change after illumination with ultraviolet light at 350 nm for 1 h, emphasizing that the nanosensor held prominent anti-photobleaching property. After storage for 120 days at 4 °C, FL intensity of the nanosensor (Fig. S11B) was 92.87% of the initial signal, which meant that the as-constructed nanosensor enjoyed favorable long-term stability. The robust photostability was ascribed to the modification of MIL-101 and the protection of imprinting layer. As illustrated in Fig. S11C, Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP maintained a stable fluorescence intensity even if the temperature rose to 60 ℃, manifesting that the as-prepared nanosensor had a good thermal stability.

Reusability acted as the essential sensing performance was appraised by repeatedly detecting fluorescence changes of Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP during five removal-rebinding cycles. As depicted in Fig. S12A, FL intensity of the nanosensor illustrated an insignificant reduction after five consecutive uses, underlining that the developed nanosensor can be reused at least 5 times. Meanwhile, the TEM image (Fig. S12B) revealed that the nanosensor maintained the original core-shell structure and distinct morphology after five cycles, denoting the desirable reusability of the nanosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of OTC in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability of the designed nanosensor was explored by selecting the commercial milk as real samples. Since OTC was not detected in the blank milk sample through the proposed fluorescent approach and HPLC-UV method, a spike and recovery experiment was implemented by introducing appropriate concentrations of OTC to samples to further determine the feasibility of sensing system. As shown in Table S5, the established fluorescence sensing approach presented satisfactory recoveries (91.20–96.19%) with a relative standard deviation less than 3.1%, which was corresponding to the results of HPLC-UV method executed in conformity to GB/T 22990–2008. The data implied that the constructed Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP-based sensing system not only can effectively avoid unnecessary and expensive sample pre-processing, but also can be well adapted to meet the requirements for accurate measurements of OTC in real samples.

The sensing performance of fluorescent approach based on Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP was compared with the previously reported methods for OTC detection, which was listed in Table S6. Apparently, the established fluorescent approach in this work displayed a lower LOD (16.8 ng mL−1), wider linear range (0.05–40 μg mL−1), shorter response time (15 min), comparable recovery and precision (RSD), and superior reusability. The distinct superiorities of the fluorescent approach were ascribed to the following aspects. First of all, MIL-101 with high porosity and large surface area, as a supporter, was beneficial to boosting the adsorption capacity and mass transfer rate of the developed nanosensor, shortening the response time and achieving signal amplification. Secondly, as the signal tag, ultra-sensitive Mg,N-CDs were incorporated into MIL-101, which inhibited the self-quenching of Mg,N-CDs and evidently improved the sensitivity of the nanosensor. Simultaneously, the ultra-small size of Mg,N-CDs facilitated its efficient encapsulation, overcoming the multi-step modification of the nanosensor. Thirdly, the modification of high-affinity imprinting sites enabled the nanosensor to enjoy superior stability and specificity, which can not only enhance the accuracy of nanosensor in identifying OTC, but also avoid the time-consuming preprocessing in practical application. Additionally, Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP composites were easily recycled from the assay system by centrifugation and washing, revealing that the nanosensor had satisfactory reusability. Benefiting from the integration of inherent advantages of MIL-101, Mg,N-CDs and imprinting layer, Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP-based fluorescent approach was favorable for rapid and high-efficiency determination of trace OTC in complex systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of smartphone-assisted optosensing platform for onsite determination of OTC
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that the proposed Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP-based fluorescence approach for sensitive detection of OTC was often subjected to expensive and bulky fluorophotometer, herein, we designed and manufactured an instrument-free intelligent optosensing platform through coupling Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP with a homemade optical equipment based on a smartphone (Fig. S1). The facile equipment containing a UV lamp with 365 nm, sample slot and optical filter, as the carrier and darkroom, not only eliminated the influence generated by the distance between sample and excitation beam, but also effectively suppressed the interference yielded by the excitation beam and environment, enhancing the accuracy and feasibility of onsite monitoring. More delightfully, as both fluorescence signal reader and substance content analyzer, the smartphone can instantly extract the RGB values from the fluorescent color images and then convert them into the corresponding I mean values, accomplishing the precise onsite detection of OTC. As delineated in Fig. 5A, Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP was first exposed to various concentrations of OTC standards. After sufficient incubation for 15 min at ambient temperature, the sensing system was transferred to the sample slot of the facile device. Under the same smartphone parameters and a LED lamp (365 nm), a series of concentration-dependent fluorescence images (Fig. 5B) was acquired by a built-in camera of the smartphone and further converted to RGB values through the professional Color Picker APP. As presented in Fig. 5C, an excellent linear relationship of I mean versus the concentration of OTC was obtained within the range from 0.09 to 40 μM, which was denoted by I mean = 0.0132 C OTC + 0.0018 (R 2 = 0.994). Meanwhile, in light of the standard 3σ/slope, the LOD was determined to be 27.3 ng mL−1. Evidently, the sensitivity of the proposed portable optosensing platform was lower than MRLs of OTC set by China and the EU, which was highly desirable for the requirements of safety control and food quality. Furthermore, the slope of the linear relationship obtained via smartphone-integrated portable optosensing platform was very close to that of the linear relationship between FL quenching degree and OTC concentration (Fig. 5D) and that of the linear relationship between HPLC peak area and OTC concentration (Fig. S13), manifesting that the as-developed portable optosensing platform based on smartphone provided a sensitive and reliable method for precise onsite analysis of OTC.

To appraise the practicability of the smartphone-based sensing strategy, the portable optosensing platform was employed to measure OTC in milk samples. The correlated results were summarized in Table S7. Delightfully, the favorable recoveries (90.86–95.41%) and a satisfactory relative standard deviation (< 4.0) were attained by utilizing the smartphone-based optosensing platform, which was comparable to the results obtained by Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP-based fluorescence method and HPLC approach. The before-mentioned results verified that the as-constructed smartphone-assisted optosensing platform has promising applications for OTC analysis.

In conclusion, a novel fluorescence biomimetic sensing nanosensor, Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP, was successfully developed, which achieved ultrasensitive and fast detection of OTC through efficient IFE. Thanks to the integration of prominent merits from Mg,N-CDs, MIL-101 and imprinting layer, the as-synthesized Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP-based fluorescence nanosensor presented a good linear range of 0.05–40 μg mL−1 μM, with a sensitive detection limit of 16.8 ng mL−1, which supplied high accessibility to realize sensitive and specific monitoring of OTC. The favorable realistic application of the proposed nanosensor was finally verified through measuring milk samples. On the other hand, by coupling Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP with a portable smartphone-integrated optical device, an intelligent optosensing platform was triumphantly constructed to satisfy the requirements for accurate onsite profiling of OTC. As sample-to-answer analyzers, the smartphone can instantly capture the concentration-dependent fluorescent images and then convert them into the corresponding RGB values. The introduction of the portable optical device effectively avoided the interference induced by environmental fluctuations, enhancing accuracy and feasibility of the nanosensing. It is more exciting that the proposed portable smartphone-assisted optosensing platform was successfully utilized for the visual measurement of OTC in real samples with satisfactory results. On account of the appealing advantages of the intelligent sensing platform, including simplicity, portability, faster processing speed and cost-effectiveness, the current work not only furnishes a promising avenue for the reasonable design of the high-quality portable device, but also presents a novel viewpoint for constructing the sensing platform for accurate point-of-care determination of various pollutants.

Oxytetracycline (OTC), as a broad-spectrum antibiotic, is often excessively or illegally added to animal feeding process to treat bacterial infections, posing a significant threat to human health and environmental safety. A core-shell fluorescence biomimetic nanosensor (Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP) that integrates ultrasensitive fluorescent response, high surface area and porosity, prominent anti-interference capacity was developed. Simultaneously, by integrating Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP with a portable smartphone-assisted optical device, an intelligent optosensing platform was triumphantly constructed to satisfy the requirements for accurate onsite profiling of OTC in milk samples. The established platform furnishes a promising avenue for simple, rapid, sensitive and instant monitoring of various pollutants.

Xuelian Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Yichuan Cao: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Lin Cai: Writing – review & editing. Haiyang Wang: Investigation, Validation. Guozhen Fang: Supervision. Shuo Wang: Supervision.
